title: Politics in the Gilded Age
description: Practice your skills in this exercise on politics in the Gilded Age.

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the excerpt and answer the question below. 

>“We protest, and with all reverence, that it is not God’s fault. We protest that it is not the farmer’s fault. We believe, and so charge, solemnly and deliberately, that it is the fault of the financial system of the Government—a system that placed on agriculture an undue, unjust and intolerable proportion of the burdens of taxation.” 

>-Source: Leonidas L. Polk, to the United States Senate Committee on Agriculture, 1890

**Which of the following issues of the period was the author most likely concerned within the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the economic instability throughout the 1880s
the urbanization of the United States in the 1890s
the spread of nativist sentiment in the 1870s and 1880s
the emergence of the Populist Party in the 1890s

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the excerpt and answer the question below. 

>“We protest, and with all reverence, that it is not God’s fault. We protest that it is not the farmer’s fault. We believe, and so charge, solemnly and deliberately, that it is the fault of the financial system of the Government—a system that placed on agriculture an undue, unjust and intolerable proportion of the burdens of taxation.” 

>-Source: Leonidas L. Polk, to the United States Senate Committee on Agriculture, 1890

**Ideas similar to those expressed in the passage directly contributed to which of the following developments?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the formation of a political party calling for increased government regulation of the economy
the enactment of tariffs to support domestic industries, including agriculture and manufacturing
the passage of antitrust legislation to regulate railroad prices and prevent unethical business practices
the sale of cheap land to settlers willing to settle in the western half of the United States

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the excerpt and answer the question below. 

>“There is something radically wrong in our industrial system. There is a screw loose. The wheels have dropped out of balance. The railroads have never been so prosperous, and yet agriculture languishes. The banks have never done a better or more profitable business, and yet agriculture languishes. Manufacturing enterprises never made more money or were in a more flourishing condition, and yet agriculture languishes. Towns and cities flourish and ‘boom’ and grow and ‘boom,’ and yet agriculture languishes. Salaries and fees were never so temptingly high and desirable, and yet agriculture languishes.” 

>-A Progressive Farmer, in a North Carolina Farm Journal, 1887

**The key concern that this excerpt was designed to address was the:**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: continued decline in the price of crops.
lack of government regulation of businesses.
complaints about the rise in immigration.
demand for government-provided education.

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the excerpt and answer the question below. 

>“There is something radically wrong in our industrial system. There is a screw loose. The wheels have dropped out of balance. The railroads have never been so prosperous, and yet agriculture languishes. The banks have never done a better or more profitable business, and yet agriculture languishes. Manufacturing enterprises never made more money or were in a more flourishing condition, and yet agriculture languishes. Towns and cities flourish and ‘boom’ and grow and ‘boom,’ and yet agriculture languishes. Salaries and fees were never so temptingly high and desirable, and yet agriculture languishes.” 

>-A Progressive Farmer, in a North Carolina Farm Journal, 1887

**Based on the excerpt, the author would most likely support which of the following?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: government regulation of railroad prices
using party patronage to fill federal positions
increasing tariff rates on imports from Europe
adhering to the gold standard as a monetary system

assessment_items.question_content:
Examine the image and answer the question below. 

![Political cartoon contrasting the economic situation of a family under bimetallism vs. monometalism. Under bimetallism, the father is employed at a bustling factory and his daughter is well-dressed and happy. Under monometalism, the factory has closed and the family is dressed in rags, begging on the street. ](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/c6db195de8a25b40a7908db840878e75.png)

>-Source: W.H.Harvey, "Take your choice," [Wikimedia Commons](https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Take_your_choice.png), 1894.

**Which of the following groups would have been most likely to support the artist’s views shown in the image?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Populists
Know-Nothings
Republicans
Nativists

assessment_items.question_content:
Examine the image and answer the question below. 

![Political cartoon contrasting the economic situation of a family under bimetallism vs. monometalism. Under bimetallism, the father is employed at a bustling factory and his daughter is well-dressed and happy. Under monometalism, the factory has closed and the family is dressed in rags, begging on the street. ](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/c6db195de8a25b40a7908db840878e75.png)

>-Source: W.H.Harvey, "Take your choice," [Wikimedia Commons](https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Take_your_choice.png), 1894.

**The policy suggested in the image was most directly a response to which of the following?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the demonetization of silver coins in the 1870s
the American commitment to building up the military in the 1880s
the acquisition of new territories in the Pacific Rim in the 1880s
the failure of labor unions to fix problems in factories in the 1890s

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the excerpts and answer the question below. 

**SOURCE 1:**

>“We must make a departure. Instead of laying on the burdens of taxation upon the necessaries of life, instead of destroying our foreign commerce, we should encourage it as we would encourage our home commerce. We should remove every unnecessary burden.”

>-Source: Democratic Congressman Roger Q. Mills, *Congressional Record,* 1888


**SOURCE 2:**

>“We are uncompromisingly in favor of the American system of protection; we protest against its destruction as proposed by the President and his party. They serve the interests of Europe; we will support the interests of America.”

>-Source: Republican Party Platform, 1888

**The excerpts most directly reflect which of the following trends in the late nineteenth century?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the growing divisions between political parties over economic policy
the influence of political machines on national social policy issues
the rise of bimetallism advocacy to address financial difficulties
the inability of Congress to effect any real change in economic policy

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the excerpts and answer the question below. 

**SOURCE 1:**

>“We must make a departure. Instead of laying on the burdens of taxation upon the necessaries of life, instead of destroying our foreign commerce, we should encourage it as we would encourage our home commerce. We should remove every unnecessary burden.”

>-Source: Democratic Congressman Roger Q. Mills, *Congressional Record,* 1888


**SOURCE 2:**

>“We are uncompromisingly in favor of the American system of protection; we protest against its destruction as proposed by the President and his party. They serve the interests of Europe; we will support the interests of America.”

>-Source: Republican Party Platform, 1888

**At the time the excerpts were written, Congressman Mills disagreed with the Republican Party platform in that Mills believed that:**

[[☃ radio 2]]

choices.content: tariffs hurt the American economy.
tariffs protect American commerce.
tariffs encourage foreign trade.
tariffs increase demand for American goods.

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the excerpts and answer the question below. 

**SOURCE 1:**

>“We must make a departure. Instead of laying on the burdens of taxation upon the necessaries of life, instead of destroying our foreign commerce, we should encourage it as we would encourage our home commerce. We should remove every unnecessary burden.”

>-Source: Democratic Congressman Roger Q. Mills, *Congressional Record,* 1888


**SOURCE 2:**

>“We are uncompromisingly in favor of the American system of protection; we protest against its destruction as proposed by the President and his party. They serve the interests of Europe; we will support the interests of America.”

>-Source: Republican Party Platform, 1888

**The policies expressed in the excerpts contributed most directly to debates in the United States about the:**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: role of foreign trade in supporting the economy.
coinage of silver to decrease the price of goods.
acquisition of territories to obtain their raw materials.
government’s regulation of business practices.

assessment_items.question_content:
Examine the image and answer the question below. 

![Nast political cartoon](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/13825b7a02ef1132c69ce8f4778510c7.jpg)
*Description: Cartoon depicting a giant thumb crushing New York City, with a cufflink reading William M. Tweed. The caption reads: "The Boss. 'Well, what are you going to do about it?'"* 

>-Source: Thomas Nast, “Under the Thumb,” *Harper’s Weekly,* [The Smithsonian](https://learninglab.si.edu/collections/thomas-nast-tammany-hall/0w0s240fBeeq4MUM#r/79654), 1871.

**The image most closely reflects which of the following developments in the political climate in the United States?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the rise of political machines in major cities
the government’s intervention in the economy
the influence of monopolists on government policy
the calls for civil service reform in the government

assessment_items.question_content:
Examine the image and answer the question below. 

![Nast cartoon](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/13825b7a02ef1132c69ce8f4778510c7.jpg)
*Cartoon depicting a giant thumb crushing New York City, with a cufflink reading William M. Tweed. The caption reads: "The Boss. 'Well, what are you going to do about it?'"* Thomas Nast, “Under the Thumb,” *Harper’s Weekly,* [The Smithsonian](https://learninglab.si.edu/collections/thomas-nast-tammany-hall/0w0s240fBeeq4MUM#r/79654), 1871.

**Which of the following arguments did some late-nineteenth century leaders use to justify the situation depicted in the image?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The leaders of political machines provided immigrants with social services that they needed to survive.
The political machines took advantage of immigrants to influence federal policies unfairly.
The leaders of political machines represented disenfranchised agricultural workers in rural areas.
The political machines undermined democracy by corrupting local, state, and federal elections.

assessment_items.question_content:
Examine the image and answer the question below. 

![Nast cartoon](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/13825b7a02ef1132c69ce8f4778510c7.jpg)
*Cartoon depicting a giant thumb crushing New York City, with a cufflink reading William M. Tweed. The caption reads: "The Boss. 'Well, what are you going to do about it?'"* Thomas Nast, “Under the Thumb,” *Harper’s Weekly,* [The Smithsonian](https://learninglab.si.edu/collections/thomas-nast-tammany-hall/0w0s240fBeeq4MUM#r/79654), 1871.

**Which of the following statements best describes the perspective expressed in the image about politics in the nineteenth century?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Political machines had an unprecedented amount of control over local politics.
Political machines were a necessary evil to improve the lives of immigrants.
Political machines facilitated the growth of monopolies in manufacturing centers.
Political machines did not influence national politics and were mostly ineffective.
